div.head: 安岳縣臥佛院A區與B區概覽
雷 德 侯
空間分佈
div.p: 沿安岳縣臥佛院山谷南側向東而行，會發現岩壁上有一些掩映於葱鬱的植被之下的洞眼。這些用於放置木樑的卯眼，是早期建築活動的痕跡。 但實際的建築結構至今則無一存留。在崖壁的西邊，即第一部分，刻有三個浮雕形式的墓塔，其中的兩座迄今尚未引起關注。 它們都在山谷對面的巨型臥佛像的視線所及範圍之內。最西邊的墓塔25-1號（） 與29號窟相距49米。頂端的錐形飾物，突起於凹陷的背景之中，儼然可見；另有一根水平的條帶位於隆起的塔身上部；最顯眼的是下方的圓形孔洞，用於盛放僧侶的骨灰。 整個結構的大小約為高180釐米，寬80釐米。
浮雕式墓塔25-2號刊鑿在29號窟西南方約25米處的崖壁上 （）。該墓塔塔頂呈三角形， 塔頂上方是一個圓形飾物，塔身呈方形，坐落在以水平條帶標示的基座上。塔身中有安放骨灰的圓孔。 整體高83釐米，寬62釐米。
墓塔25號在25-2號墓塔往東過去約10米處，以減地浮雕形式刻鑿於崖壁上一塊開闊的平面上 （）。與25-2號墓塔相同，三角形塔頂的上方冠以一個寶珠頂飾。方形的塔身安坐於一個三層階梯的臺基上。 臺基下方是一個盛放骨灰的圓形孔洞。整個結構大小約為高100釐米，寬80釐米。
臥佛頭部的正對面，在通往25號墓塔的低地之旁，有一塊凸起的石面即是A區刻經窟所在之處（）。該區西端近地面處是小型的26號龕。其上方為27號和28號龕，兩龕左側又緊鄰著29號窟（；；）。

A區的兩個刻經窟是一個由五部分組成的對稱結構的一部分。 位於中央的31號龕內有一組七尊的群像，包括一尊坐佛，其兩邊分别排列著一弟子、一菩薩和一力士，兩兩對稱（）。此龕的兩邊各一尊大型佛像，西邊為一尊結跏趺坐於蓮座上的坐佛（30號龕；）；東邊則是雙足懸垂於方座上的倚坐佛（32號龕）。這兩尊雕像外側，各有一個刻經窟，西邊是29號窟，東邊是33號窟。這種五重對稱的布局在後面的石窟中沒有再出現過。這一將佛教經文與雕像結合於一個整體的組合， 雖然一開始未必面面俱到，但不失為獨特的嘗試。

figure.head: A區和B 區平面圖及立面圖
Ground plan and elevation of sections A and B

div.head: 刻經洞窟
div.p: A區的兩個刻經窟凡刻八部佛經：一部在29號窟內，一部刊於其外，其餘六部均刻於33號窟內。 29號窟內刻《佛說佛名經》十二品中的四品，刻滿三個壁面，是A區最長的一篇刻經。如同1號與2號窟，29號窟也是全窟僅刻一部佛經的例子。
29號窟外i壁《心經》顯然刊刻得較晚，並不在原本的設計之中。該經因其篇幅短小，特別受供養人喜愛，因为他們不用破費太多就可以供養一部完整的佛經。 71號窟d壁也刻有此經，本叢書將另卷討論。由於29號窟外的《心經》為晚期增刻，將不列入以下關於兩窟刻經安排的討論範圍。
開窟者在 33 號窟遇到了岩石空間本身的問題，或許給他們增加了難度。此窟進深格外淺。相比於 29 號窟的 d 壁進深248釐米， 33 號窟中 d 壁進深僅為190釐米。後牆 e 壁的中央部分，岩石剝落，且壁面潮濕。這樣的地質情況可能限制了石匠當初向岩石的更深處開鑿。

figure.head: 遠眺A區和B 區；2010年拍攝
Sections A and B from afar; photograph taken in 2010

div.p: 33號窟內的六部佛經以《灌頂經》第十二卷和第十一卷開始（按此序刊刻），刻於整個d壁以及e壁前一部分。此兩卷經文均以“聞如是”開頭，卷次數“十二”和“十一”似未刊刻上石，因此，可視為兩部獨立的佛經。後面的佛經分別是：位於e壁的《六門陀羅尼經》和《佛臨般涅槃略說遺教經》，位於f壁的《佛說出家功德經》和《金剛經》。這四部經文都是完整的。
A區所有刻經有著顯著的共同特徵，即是其踐行性與儀軌用途。《佛說佛名經》即屬於此類佛經。此經中，佛名與穿插其中的懺悔文被用於懺悔儀式，並有經文詳述如何舉行這些儀式。29號窟《佛說佛名經》雖無懺悔文貫穿其中，但顯然也用於懺悔儀式。

figure.head: A區（圖中右側為西）；2012年拍攝
Section A; the west is to the right in the photograph taken in 2012

div.p: 十二卷《灌頂經》講的是如何通過儀式消災避禍。第十二卷詳述治癒疾病和避免橫死的儀式；第十一卷則教導世人如何在臨死前累積功德。
《六門陀羅尼經》要求信徒每天作六次功課，念誦此經，以求精進。《佛垂般涅槃略說教誡經》則主要針對寺院團體和行乞僧。而《佛說出家功德經》是提倡並讚美寺院生活，對妨礙者予以威脅。
因此，從這六部刻經可以看出一種漸進關係，在29號窟闡明了懺悔儀式之後，33號窟首先關注世人的健康和精神福祉。 隨後是針對僧眾，繼之則是有意出家之人。最後，如溫狄婭（Claudia Wenzel）所說， 受戒者可與佛陀本人直接對話，一如《金剛經》中的佛弟子須菩提。見下文，第頁。
除了上述的經文選擇和順序，死亡是另一普遍關心的主題。《灌頂經》第十一卷述及死亡之前的準備與往生淨土，而此經宣說時間即在佛陀涅槃之前。 藏經中有數部佛經被認為是佛陀即將涅槃前最後的說法，《佛垂般涅槃略說教誡經》就是其中之一。這一重大事件又是對面臥佛的主題，兩相呼應，應當是有意的選擇。

figure.head: A區的30號龕坐佛和31號龕；2009年拍攝
Seated Buddha 30 and niche 31 of section A; photograph taken in 2009

div.head: 未刻經洞窟
div.p:
B區的四個洞窟無一竣工，因此也未刊刻佛經。第一個石窟（39號窟）的d壁與最後一個石窟（42號窟）的f壁相距約13米。它們可能按原計劃為一個石窟群，但也許因為含有軟砂岩，石質不佳而被廢棄。 不過，由於這四個窟處於不同的開鑿階段，從而提供出一幅極好的工作流程示意圖，展示了石匠在臥佛院開鑿石窟的過程（）。
第368頁；雷玉華第100－101頁，對此有詳盡的描述。

開窟工作的第一階段可從40號窟一窺究竟，這裡工程剛剛開始（）。在山崖上僅鑿出一個高約80釐米、深僅45釐米的空間。 在41號窟石匠已將工作推進到下一個階段（）。底面已完全平整，入口兩邊的壁面也已經鑿出，但窟頂仍有大塊岩石尚未開鑿，當工匠水平地鑿斷其頂部與山體的連接部分時，巨石自然會掉落。而42號窟中的垂石已經清除（），整個鑿出的空間高167釐米，深147釐米。下一步的工作可能是窟頂和底面的加工。39號窟石匠的工作幾近完成（）。該窟基本完工，僅需打磨壁面和刻經區。窟高270釐米，這一高度並不常見。
40和41號窟的入口尚未完工，崖壁上仍留有小圓孔和淺刻線，標示出計劃中入口的位置和尺寸。 而幾乎完成的42號窟，只有西側仍留有幾個小孔。這恐怕是開窟之前就已預先設計好的排水系統， 40、41和42號窟上方的排水溝和方孔，同樣也是這種情況。另外各窟下方還有些別的小孔，似乎是用於放置連接洞窟間棧道的木樑，但其確切功能尚不明瞭。
雷玉華觀察到，向東延伸的崖壁因崩坍略微凹曲，上有一條明正德四年（1509）的題記， 即“明正德四年己巳八月初三修”。雷玉華第101頁和第112頁，註6。 說明該區域在當時或更早就已風蝕。繼續向東，在靠近46號窟的崖壁上有六個圓孔，表明這裡可能曾有另外開窟的計劃，但在開工之前就放棄了。

figure.head: B區前段的39號窟（右）和40號窟；2010年拍攝
First part of section B with caves 39 (right) and 40; photograph taken in 2010

div.head: Sections A and B at the Grove of the Reclining Buddha
Lothar Ledderose
Spatial Grouping
div.p: As the visitor walks eastward along the southern side of the valley of the Grove of the Reclining Buddha (Wofoyuan 臥佛院) in Anyue County 安岳縣, he passes cavities in the rock walls hidden under the lush vegetation. These were sockets for wooden beams, traces of earlier building activity; no actual structures survive. In this western, first section of the cliff are three tomb pagodas, engraved in relief, two of which have gone unnoticed up to now. All are in the line of sight of the colossal reclining Buddha figure on the opposite side of the valley. The westernmost pagoda 25-1 (fig. ) is as much as 49 meters away from cave 29. Still visible are the cone-shaped jewel on the summit, set into an intaglio frame, a horizontal bar marking the top of the bulging body, and, most clearly, the round opening below for receiving the ashes of the monk. The entire structure measures ca. 180 x 80 cm.

Tomb pagoda 25-2, chiselled in relief into the cliff, is ca. 25 meters to the southwest of cave 29 (fig. ). It has a triangular roof surmounted by a round jewel; a square body sits on a base in the shape of a horizontal bar. In the body is the round inset for the ashes. The pagoda measures 83 cm in height, and 62 cm in width. The tomb pagoda 25 is ca. ten meters further to the east from 25-2, engraved in stronger relief on a large smooth area of the cliff (fig. ). As in 25-2, the triangular roof is surmounted by a jewel; the square body rests on a three stepped base, below which is the round opening for the ashes. The entire structure measures ca. 100 x 80 cm.

Beyond a small canyon, which leads up to the top of the cliff above pagoda 25, the bulging rock face accommodates the sutra caves of section A (fig. ), exactly opposite the head of the reclining Buddha. On the ground level at the western edge is the small shrine 26. Higher up, to the left adjacent to cave 29, are shrines 27 and 28 (fig. ; fig. ; fig. ).

The two sutra caves in this section A are part of a ensemble of five units. The center niche 31 is filled with a group of seven figures; a seated Buddha, and, on each side, a disciple, a bodhisattva, and a guardian figure (fig. ). On both sides of this niche are Buddha sculptures: to the west, a figure sitting on a lotus throne, niche 30 (fig. ), and, to the east, a seated figure with pendant legs, niche 32. Beyond these sculptures, on either side, are the two sutra caves, cave 29 to the west, and cave 33 to the east. Such a five-fold symmetrical arrangement does not occur again among the caves in the following sections. This group was a unique effort to combine sutra texts and sculptures in an integrated design, even if the plan may not have been laid out in every detail from the start.

div.head: Caves with Sutras
div.p: The two caves in section A present eight sutra texts, one inside cave 29, one on its outside, and six in cave 33. The text inside cave 29, with four of the twelve chapters of the Sutra on the Names of the Buddhas, is the longest of them all, covering three walls. As in caves 1 and 2, this is an instance, where just one sutra alone fills a cave completely.

figure.head: 26號龕（右下方）、27號龕和28號龕（鄰近29號窟窟門）；2010年拍攝
Shrines 26 (lower right), 27 and 28, next to the gate of cave 29; photograph taken in 2010

div.p: The Heart Sutra on the outside of cave 29, on its wall i, was apparently engraved at a later date and was not part of the original scheme. Because it is so short, the sutra was favored by donors, who, without making a large contribution, intended to donate at least one entire sutra. The sutra is also engraved at the Grove in cave 71, wall d, and will be discussed in a later volume. Because the Heart Sutra outside cave 29 is a later addition, it is also not taken into account in the following remarks about the arrangement of the texts in the two caves.

In cave 33 the makers encountered a problem with the rock itself that may have exacerbated the difficulties that they faced. The cave is unusually shallow. Whereas in cave 29, wall d extends 248 cm towards the rear, in cave 33, wall d is only 190 cm in depth. In a central area of the rear wall e, the stone is flaking off in layers and is moist. The geological faults may have caused the carvers to desist from cutting deeper into the rock.

The six texts in cave 33 start, on wall d and the beginning of wall e, with scrolls 12 and 11 of the Consecration Sutra (in this reversed order). The two scrolls each begin with the phrase “I have heard thus (wen rushi 聞如是)”, and the scroll numbers 12 and 11 appear not to be engraved. Thereby the two texts are accorded the status of independent sutras. Then follow four more sutras in their entirety: on wall e, the Dhāraṇī Sutra of the Six Gates, and the Deathbed Injunction Sutra, and, on wall f, the Sutra on Renouncing the Householder’s Life and the Diamond Sutra.

figure.head: 墓塔25；2012年拍攝
Tomb pagoda 25; photograph taken in 2012

div.p: A conspicuous common feature of all texts in section A is their performative character and liturgical use. The Sutra on the Names of the Buddhas is one of a great variety of such similar texts. Buddha names with interspersed repentance passages were invoked in rituals of confession, and some texts explain how these rituals were to be performed. The version in cave 29 does not have repentance passages between the Buddha names, but was obviously also used in confession rituals.

figure.head: 墓塔25-1；2012年拍攝
Tomb pagoda 25-1; photograph taken in 2012

div.p: The Dhāraṇī Sutra of the Six Gates requires its ritual recitation six times during day and night in pursuit of spiritual advancement. The Deathbed Injunction Sutra is directed toward the monastic community and to mendicant monks, and the Sutra on Renouncing the Householderʼs Life advocates and praises the merits of monastic life, and threatens those who obstruct.

One can thus recognize a progression in these six texts. After the repentance rituals have been laid out in cave 29, cave 33 begins with the concern for health and spiritual well-being of laymen; thereafter, the texts first turn to monks and then to those who will become monks. Finally, as Claudia Wenzel has pointed out,Below, p. . the ordained can enter into a direct dialogue with the Buddha himself, alongside his disciple Subhūti, who figures as the Buddha’s interlocutor in the Diamond Sutra.

In addition to this structure in the choice and sequence of the engraved texts, death is a common theme. Scroll 11 of the Consecration Sutra addresses the preparation for death and rebirth in the Pure Land, and it is preached by the Buddha before his nirvana. The Deathbed Injunction Sutra, too, is one of several texts in the canon that claim to be the Buddha’s last sermon as he is about to make the transition into nirvana. This seminal event is powerfully enacted and made visible in the giant sculpture directly opposite the caves of section A. Its sutras have been chosen in keeping with the overarching theme of the Grove of the Reclining Buddha.

figure.head: 墓塔25-2；2012年拍攝
Tomb pagoda 25-2; photograph taken in 2012
29號窟外牆i旁的30號龕及坐佛像；2010年拍攝
Niche 30 with seated Buddha figure, next to exterior wall i of sutra cave 29; photograph taken in 2010

div.head: Caves without Sutras
div.p: The four caves of section B were never finished, and so none of them contains engraved sutra texts. The distance from wall d of the first cave, cave 39, to wall f of the last cave, cave 42, is ca. 13 meters. They were probably intended to be a group, but were perhaps abandoned because of the poor quality of the rock, which includes layers of a rather soft sandstone. Yet, as the four caves have been left in four different stages of completion, they provide a welcome illustration of the working methods that the masons followed in the Grove of the Reclining Buddha when creating their caves (fig. ). This process is observed and described in 368; and in 100–101.

We see the first stage at cave 40, where work has only just begun (fig. ). A recess is cut into the cliff wall to a height of ca. 80 cm, but to a depth of merely 45 cm. At cave 41 the masons have taken the work to a further stage (fig. ). The floor is already completely even, as are the side walls near the entrance. At the center, a big chunk of rock remains hanging down from the ceiling. It would have been allowed to fall piece by piece, by severing the rock horizontally to chisel out the ceiling. In cave 42, the hanging rock has been removed (fig. ). The cave is hollowed out to a height of 167 cm and a depth of 147 cm. Further work was probably planned for the ceiling as well as for the floor. The masons had almost completed the work at cave 39 (fig. ). It is substantially finished, ready for the polishing of the walls and of the fields of script. Unusual is its height of 270 cm.

In caves 40 and 41, whose entrances were not opened to their full extent, small circular holes and shallow lines can be identified on the cliff. These mark the positions and sizes of the proposed entrances of the caves, to direct the masons to hollow them out. At the nearly finished cave 42, only on its western side do a few small holes remain. A drainage system may have been planned and carved before the work of hollowing out began, as witnessed by channels and square holes above caves 40, 41, and 42. Underneath all the caves are more small holes, probably used for fixing wooden beams for walkways between the caves and other purposes, but their precise function is unclear.

As Lei Yuhua has observed, on the cliff surface further to the east, which collapsed forming a slightly concave bend, is a colophon dated 1509.“明正德四年已巳八月初三修”. 101, 112, note 6. It indicates that the area was already eroded on or before this date. Still further to the east, on the cliff surface near cave 46, are six circular holes, suggesting that another cave may have been planned for this position, but was abandoned before construction began.

figure.head: 41號窟（右）和42號窟；2010年拍攝
Caves 41 (right) and 42; photograph taken in 2010
